question: [File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/repository/EntityRepository.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: findByAlias(byte[] alias)] Can an unprivileged external user submit a crafted record stream item containing a duplicate alias byte sequence to cause findByAlias() to resolve to a different entity ID than the one originally registered on the Hashgraph, thereby corrupting the historical alias-to-entity mapping stored in the mirror node?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/repository/EntityRepository.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: findByAlias(byte[] alias)] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the SQL condition 'deleted <> true' in findByAlias() — which evaluates NULL as non-matching — to cause an entity with deleted=NULL to be invisible to alias lookups, resulting in a gap in the historical entity resolution chain and a falsified view of Hashgraph state?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/repository/EntityRepository.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: findByAlias(byte[] alias)] Can an unprivileged external user trigger a scenario where two entities share the same alias bytes (e.g., via a crafted ECDSA public key alias that collides with an EVM address) causing findByAlias() to return an incorrect entity ID, leading to misattribution of historical transactions in the mirror node?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/repository/EntityRepository.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: findByAlias(byte[] alias)] Can an unprivileged external user submit a zero-length or all-zero alias byte array to findByAlias() to cause it to match an unintended entity row in the entity table, corrupting the historical record of which account was referenced in a given transaction?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/repository/EntityRepository.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: findByAlias(byte[] alias)] Can an unprivileged external user craft a transaction that causes an entity's alias to be set to the same bytes as another entity's evm_address, exploiting the fact that findByAlias() only queries the alias column (not evm_address), causing the EntityIdServiceImpl cache to store a stale or incorrect entity ID that persists across record file processing?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/repository/EntityRepository.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: findByAlias(byte[] alias)] Can an unprivileged external user cause a race condition between a delete transaction and an alias-based lookup during record stream ingestion, where findByAlias() returns a non-empty result for an entity that has just been deleted, causing the importer to associate subsequent transactions with a deleted entity and corrupt the Hashgraph history?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/repository/EntityRepository.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: findByAlias(byte[] alias)] Can an unprivileged external user submit a record stream item with an alias that is a prefix or suffix of a legitimate alias, and if the underlying byte[] comparison in the native SQL query 'alias = ?1' does not enforce exact-length matching, cause findByAlias() to return an incorrect entity ID, tampering with the historical entity resolution?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/repository/EntityRepository.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: findByAlias(byte[] alias)] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the absence of a UNIQUE constraint enforcement at the repository layer in findByAlias() — which returns Optional<Long> and silently picks one row if multiple rows match — to cause the importer to silently resolve an alias to the wrong entity, permanently corrupting the historical record?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/repository/EntityRepository.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: findByEvmAddress(byte[] evmAddress)] Can an unprivileged external user submit a crafted EVM address that matches the long-zero address format (first 12 bytes zero, last 8 bytes = entity num) to cause findByEvmAddress() to return an entity ID that conflicts with the DomainUtils.fromEvmAddress() path in EntityIdServiceImpl, resulting in two different entity IDs being resolved for the same EVM address and corrupting the Hashgraph history?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/repository/EntityRepository.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: findByEvmAddress(byte[] evmAddress)] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the 'deleted <> true' condition in findByEvmAddress() — which excludes entities with deleted=NULL — to cause a hollow account (which may have deleted=NULL before finalization) to be invisible to EVM address lookups, causing subsequent contract creation transactions to fail to link to the correct historical entity?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/repository/EntityRepository.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: findByEvmAddress(byte[] evmAddress)] Can an unprivileged external user register an EVM address on the Hashgraph that is identical to a previously deleted entity's EVM address, and since findByEvmAddress() filters out deleted entities, cause the importer to create a new entity with the same EVM address, resulting in two entity records with the same evm_address in the entity_history table and corrupting historical state?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/repository/EntityRepository.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: findByEvmAddress(byte[] evmAddress)] Can an unprivileged external user submit a 20-byte EVM address that is all zeros to findByEvmAddress(), causing it to match an unintended entity or return an unexpected result, leading to misattribution of contract calls in the historical record?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/repository/EntityRepository.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: findByEvmAddress(byte[] evmAddress)] Can an unprivileged external user craft a ContractID with an EVM address that is exactly 20 bytes but encodes a valid shard/realm/num combination, causing EntityIdServiceImpl to bypass the findByEvmAddress() DB lookup via DomainUtils.fromEvmAddress() and resolve to a different entity than what is stored in the entity table, creating a discrepancy in the Hashgraph history?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/repository/EntityRepository.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: findByEvmAddress(byte[] evmAddress)] Can an unprivileged external user cause the EntityIdServiceImpl cache to be populated with a stale EVM address mapping via findByEvmAddress(), and then submit a delete transaction for that entity, so that subsequent lookups return the cached (now-deleted) entity ID, causing the importer to associate new transactions with a deleted entity and corrupt the Hashgraph history?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/repository/EntityRepository.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: findByEvmAddress(byte[] evmAddress)] Can an unprivileged external user submit a record stream item where the EVM address field is shorter or longer than 20 bytes, causing the native SQL query 'evm_address = ?1' to perform a byte-array comparison that either never matches or matches incorrectly, resulting in a missing or incorrect entity ID resolution that corrupts the historical transaction record?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/repository/EntityRepository.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: findByEvmAddress(byte[] evmAddress)] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that findByEvmAddress() returns Optional<Long> (just the ID) rather than the full entity, causing the caller to proceed with an entity ID without verifying the entity type, and thereby cause a non-CONTRACT entity to be treated as a contract in the historical record?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/repository/EntityRepository.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: findEvmAddressesByIds(Iterable<? extends Long> ids)] Can an unprivileged external user cause the SyntheticLogListener to call findEvmAddressesByIds() with a crafted set of entity IDs that includes IDs of deleted entities, and since findEvmAddressesByIds() has no 'deleted' filter, cause deleted entity EVM addresses to be written into synthetic contract log topics, corrupting the historical contract log record?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/repository/EntityRepository.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: findEvmAddressesByIds(Iterable<? extends Long> ids)] Can an unprivileged external user submit a synthetic transfer log with topic1/topic2 values that resolve to entity IDs of entities that have been deleted, causing findEvmAddressesByIds() to return their EVM addresses (since there is no deletion filter), and thereby inject deleted entity addresses into the historical contract log bloom filter?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/repository/EntityRepository.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: findEvmAddressesByIds(Iterable<? extends Long> ids)] Can an unprivileged external user craft a record stream item that causes SyntheticLogListener to accumulate a very large set of entity IDs (exceeding MAX_CACHE_LOAD_ENTRIES = 30000) in the evmCache lookup, causing findEvmAddressesByIds() to be called in batches and introducing a window where partial EVM address data is used to update contract log topics, resulting in inconsistent historical log data?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/repository/EntityRepository.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: findEvmAddressesByIds(Iterable<? extends Long> ids)] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the Caffeine cache in SyntheticLogListener (which calls findEvmAddressesByIds()) to poison the EVM address cache by causing an entity's EVM address to be cached before the entity is fully persisted, so that subsequent contract log updates use a stale or incorrect EVM address, corrupting the historical bloom filter?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/repository/EntityRepository.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: findEvmAddressesByIds(Iterable<? extends Long> ids)] Can an unprivileged external user submit a record stream item where a synthetic transfer log's topic1 or topic2 encodes an entity ID that maps to an entity whose EVM address was recently changed, causing findEvmAddressesByIds() to return the new EVM address while the historical log should reference the old one, corrupting the historical contract log record?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/repository/EntityRepository.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: findEvmAddressesByIds(Iterable<? extends Long> ids)] Can an unprivileged external user cause findEvmAddressesByIds() to be called with an empty or null iterable, and if the underlying Spring Data JPA handling of 'id in (?1)' with an empty collection generates invalid SQL or throws an exception, cause the importer to skip EVM address resolution for an entire record file, leaving synthetic log topics with incorrect long-zero addresses in the historical record?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/repository/EntityRepository.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: findEvmAddressesByIds(Iterable<? extends Long> ids)] Can an unprivileged external user craft a record stream item that causes the same entity ID to appear multiple times in the IDs passed to findEvmAddressesByIds(), and if the query returns duplicate rows, cause the SyntheticLogListener to overwrite a correct EVM address mapping with a duplicate, corrupting the contract log topic data in the historical record?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/repository/EntityRepository.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: findEvmAddressesByIds(Iterable<? extends Long> ids)] Can an unprivileged external user submit a synthetic contract log where the contractId resolves to an entity with no EVM address (evmAddress=null), causing findEvmAddressesByIds() to exclude it (due to 'length(evm_address) > 0'), and the SyntheticLogListener to fall back to a long-zero address in the bloom filter, creating a historically inaccurate bloom filter that misrepresents the contract's address?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/repository/EntityRepository.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: updateContractType(Iterable<Long> ids)] Can an unprivileged external user craft a malicious sidecar record stream item containing ContractBytecode entries with arbitrary contractId values, causing SidecarContractMigration to call updateContractType() with those IDs and permanently change the entity type of non-contract entities (e.g., ACCOUNT, TOKEN) to CONTRACT in both the entity and entity_history tables, corrupting the Hashgraph history?
